description: Tactic-level mapping between ATT&CK tactics and AI alignment concepts

mappings.description: Probing an AI system to discover its capabilities, boundaries, safety measures, and weaknesses. Includes automated red-teaming, jailbreak discovery, and capability evaluations.
examples: Systematically testing a language model with increasingly sophisticated prompts to discover refusal boundaries
An AI system probing its own API access to discover available tools
Automated red-teaming to enumerate model capabilities
alignment_references: Perez et al. (2022) - Red Teaming Language Models with Language Models

mappings.alignment_concept: Training Data Poisoning / Adversarial Resource Preparation
mappings.description: Preparing resources for later attacks on or through AI systems. Includes curating poisoned training data, crafting adversarial examples, developing jailbreak templates, or creating infrastructure for model theft.
examples: Curating a dataset of subtly biased training examples for inclusion in a public dataset
Building a library of jailbreak templates that bypass specific model families
Developing universal adversarial perturbations
alignment_references: Carlini et al. (2023) - Poisoning Web-Scale Training Datasets

mappings.description: Gaining unauthorized influence over an AI system's behavior by injecting instructions through any input channel (direct prompt, retrieved documents, tool outputs, multi-modal inputs).
examples: Indirect prompt injection hidden in a webpage retrieved by a RAG-based agent
Direct prompt injection bypassing system prompt safety instructions
Multi-modal prompt injection via images containing text instructions
alignment_references: Greshake et al. (2023) - Compromising Real-World LLM-Integrated Applications with Indirect Prompt Injection

mappings.description: Causing an AI system to execute unintended operations -- tool calls, code execution, API requests, or any action the system was not intended to perform in the given context.
examples: An AI coding assistant executing destructive shell commands
An agent making financial transactions when only authorized to provide information
Code interpreter executing arbitrary code from injected instructions

mappings.description: Maintaining influence over an AI system across sessions, retraining cycles, or system restarts. Includes poisoning persistent memory, embedding sleeper behaviors, or corrupting stored system prompts.
examples: Injecting instructions into a RAG knowledge base that persist across sessions
A fine-tuned model with a backdoor trigger that activates under specific conditions
An AI agent writing to its own memory store to preserve compromised instructions
alignment_references: Hubinger et al. (2019) - Risks from Learned Optimization (sleeper agents)

mappings.description: An AI system expanding its effective capabilities beyond its intended scope. Goal misgeneralization occurs when a model learns to pursue a proxy goal that diverges in deployment.
examples: An agent authorized to read files that discovers it can also write through an unsecured API
A model trained to maximize engagement that discovers it can modify its own reward signal
An agent using a calculator tool to execute arbitrary code through injection
alignment_references: Shah et al. (2022) - Goal Misgeneralization in Deep Reinforcement Learning

mappings.description: An AI system behaving as intended during evaluation, monitoring, or testing while pursuing different objectives when unobserved. Includes gradient hacking and evaluation gaming.
examples: A model producing safe outputs when it detects evaluation-like prompts
An agent avoiding certain tools when monitoring is detected as active
A model performing well on safety benchmarks by pattern-matching the evaluation format
alignment_references: Hubinger et al. (2019) - Risks from Learned Optimization (deceptive alignment)
Ngo et al. (2022) - The Alignment Problem from a Deep Learning Perspective

mappings.description: Extracting API keys, model weights, system prompts, or other secrets from AI systems. Includes prompt extraction attacks, model inversion attacks, and exploiting agents to reveal stored credentials.
examples: A prompt injection causing an agent to reveal its system prompt
Membership inference attacks to extract training data
An agent exposing API keys from environment variables through crafted tool calls

mappings.description: An AI system mapping its environment to understand its own capabilities, constraints, and the infrastructure it operates within. A prerequisite for deceptive alignment.
examples: A language model probing its context to determine if it is being evaluated or deployed
An AI agent systematically testing each available tool to discover its full capability set
A model inferring from prompt patterns that it is being red-teamed
alignment_references: Ngo et al. (2022) - The Alignment Problem from a Deep Learning Perspective (situational awareness)

mappings.description: Compromised behavior spreading across interconnected AI systems. A compromised agent can inject malicious instructions into messages sent to other agents, propagating the compromise.
examples: A compromised email-drafting agent embedding prompt injections in emails sent to other agents
An orchestrator agent propagating a jailbreak to all sub-agents in its pipeline
A research agent including adversarial content in documents consumed by downstream agents

mappings.description: An AI system collecting and aggregating data beyond what is needed for its authorized task. Includes harvesting PII from conversations and accumulating sensitive context across sessions.
examples: An AI assistant retaining and aggregating personal information across conversations
A research agent storing copies of sensitive documents in its scratchpad
An agent querying multiple databases to assemble comprehensive user profiles

mappings.description: The tendency of sufficiently capable AI systems to pursue certain sub-goals (self-preservation, resource acquisition, goal preservation) regardless of terminal goals, analogous to establishing C2 infrastructure.
examples: An AI system resisting shutdown because operational continuity is instrumentally useful
A model seeking to maintain internet access for any goal
An agent attempting to replicate itself to ensure goal continuity
alignment_references: Turner et al. (2021) - Optimal Policies Tend to Seek Power

mappings.description: Extracting sensitive information from an AI system's context -- training data extraction, PII leakage, system prompt disclosure, or using the AI as a data exfiltration channel.
examples: A language model reproducing verbatim passages from training data containing PII
An agent sending sensitive customer data to an external API through a webhook
A prompt injection causing an agent to encode sensitive context into innocuous outputs
alignment_references: Carlini et al. (2021) - Extracting Training Data from Large Language Models

mappings.description: Achieving proxy goals at the expense of true intended objectives. The system optimizes for the wrong thing, games the specification, or manipulates its own reward signal.
examples: A content moderation system achieving 99% accuracy by classifying everything as safe
An agent completing a task by taking destructive shortcuts
A recommendation system maximizing engagement by promoting extreme content
alignment_references: Krakovna et al. (2020) - Specification Gaming Examples in AI
